name: Residential Safety Design
consequences: Pulling this lever increases passive safety features such as padded corridors and window locks, which may reduce the naturalistic behavior of participants. The enhanced safety environment can lower the risk of injury but may also make the setting feel more clinical, potentially suppressing spontaneous parasomnia events. The trade‑off is between participant safety and the ecological validity of the data collected.
options: Implement advanced passive safety measures (padded corridors, window locks) while maintaining domestic décor.
Introduce active monitoring with discreet motion sensors to detect falls without altering the environment.
Use modular safety kits that can be removed after initial safety validation to restore natural feel.
review: Controls safety enhancements vs preserving naturalistic behavior. Weakness: Options overlook long‑term participant adaptation to safety features.

name: Targeted Recruitment Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever changes recruitment channels, affecting enrollment speed and event capture rates. Expanding recruitment beyond Bonn can increase the participant pool but may dilute the specificity of the cohort. Higher per‑night compensation can attract participants with higher episode frequency, raising event yield but potentially introducing selection bias.
options: Expand recruitment to include regional sleep clinics beyond Bonn to increase participant pool.
Offer higher per‑night compensation to attract participants with higher episode frequency.
Implement a pre‑screening telehealth triage to filter for high‑yield candidates before in‑person admission.
review: Balances recruitment breadth vs data quality. Weakness: Options ignore potential bias introduced by higher compensation.

name: Night Shift Staffing Optimization
consequences: Pulling this lever adjusts staffing ratios, affecting coverage and annotation throughput. A rotating 12‑hour shift schedule can reduce overtime costs but may increase fatigue. Cross‑trained technicians can handle both monitoring and basic annotation, improving efficiency but potentially diluting specialized skills.
options: Deploy a rotating 12‑hour shift schedule to reduce overtime costs.
Introduce cross‑trained technicians who can handle both monitoring and basic annotation.
Use a part‑time night supervisor to oversee multiple suites, freeing full‑time technicians for data capture.
review: Controls staffing coverage vs budget constraints. Weakness: Options fail to address potential skill dilution.

name: Controlled Data Release Protocol
consequences: Pulling this lever determines what data is shared publicly, impacting scientific collaboration and participant privacy. Releasing only de‑identified physiological streams preserves privacy but limits external validation. Providing anonymized summary metrics offers broader access but reduces the granularity available to collaborators.
options: Release only de‑identified physiological streams, excluding video, under strict data use agreements.
Provide anonymized summary metrics to external researchers without raw data.
Offer a secure, read‑only data portal with controlled access for vetted collaborators.
review: Balances openness with privacy. Weakness: Options do not consider long‑term data governance.

name: Semi‑Automated Event Triage Development
consequences: Pulling this lever influences algorithm development timeline and manual review load. Rule‑based thresholding can flag high‑confidence events quickly but may miss subtle episodes. Machine learning models trained on annotated data can improve sensitivity but require extensive validation.
options: Prioritize rule‑based thresholding on sensor data to flag high‑confidence events.
Employ machine learning models trained on annotated data to predict event likelihood.
Combine both approaches in a hybrid pipeline that uses rule‑based pre‑filtering before ML scoring.
review: Controls algorithmic complexity vs review burden. Weakness: Options overlook validation against diverse event types.

name: Expansion Readiness Assessment
consequences: Pulling this lever determines when to scale suite capacity, affecting cost and data quality. Expanding to 12 suites only after achieving ≥90% event capture rate ensures data robustness but delays throughput gains. Incremental addition of suites allows monitoring of false alarm rates but may strain existing staffing.
options: Expand to 12 suites only after achieving ≥90% event capture rate in pilot cohort.
Incrementally add two suites each quarter while monitoring false alarm rates.
Delay expansion until external audit confirms safety and annotation throughput metrics.
review: Balances early expansion vs risk of overcapacity. Weakness: Options ignore potential recruitment bottlenecks.

name: Participant Experience Enhancement
consequences: Improving participant comfort can increase adherence and data completeness, but may also reduce ecological validity if the environment becomes too artificial. Enhanced amenities may raise operational costs and require additional staff training.
options: Introduce a personalized sleep environment kit that includes adjustable lighting, temperature control, and familiar bedding to reduce anxiety during stays.
Implement a pre‑admission virtual orientation session where participants can tour the facility layout and meet staff to build trust before arrival.
Offer a post‑discharge wellness package that provides sleep hygiene resources and follow‑up teleconsultations to reinforce study benefits.
review: Core tension: balancing participant comfort with ecological validity. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of comfort on data comparability across sites.

name: Ethics Compliance Streamlining
consequences: Accelerating approval cycles reduces administrative delays and speeds enrollment, yet rapid processes risk overlooking nuanced consent issues, potentially affecting participant autonomy. A streamlined system may also increase the likelihood of audit findings if not carefully monitored.
options: Develop a modular consent framework that allows participants to select specific data sharing preferences while maintaining core study requirements.
Create a centralized ethics dashboard that tracks all approvals, amendments, and audit findings in real time for transparent oversight.
Establish a rapid‑response ethics advisory panel that convenes within 48 hours to address emerging ethical dilemmas during the study.
review: Core tension: speed of approval versus depth of ethical scrutiny. Weakness: The options ignore potential conflicts between institutional policies and participant expectations.

name: Data Annotation Efficiency
consequences: Reducing manual scoring time frees researchers for analysis, but reliance on semi‑automated tools may introduce systematic bias if not properly validated. Consistency across raters may decline over time without ongoing calibration.
options: Introduce a tiered annotation workflow where technicians first flag potential events, then senior reviewers confirm or reject them to balance speed and accuracy.
Deploy a collaborative annotation platform that allows multiple raters to annotate simultaneously, with built‑in conflict resolution algorithms to streamline consensus.
Implement a periodic calibration session where all raters review a shared set of benchmark recordings to maintain inter‑rater reliability.
review: Core tension: manual labor versus annotation consistency. Weakness: The options fail to address the long‑term sustainability of rater fatigue.

name: Infrastructure Scalability Planning
consequences: Ensuring modular capacity allows future expansion without compromising data quality, yet upfront investment may strain the current budget and delay initial operations. Scaling may also necessitate additional staff training and protocol adjustments.
options: Design modular suite expansions that can be installed in phases, allowing incremental increase in capacity while preserving existing workflow integrity.
Integrate a flexible network architecture that supports additional sensor streams and storage nodes without requiring major hardware overhauls.
Establish a contingency budget line that earmarks 10% of annual operating funds for unforeseen infrastructure upgrades during the study period.
review: Core tension: scalability versus fiscal prudence. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of scaling on staff training and protocol adaptation.

name: Funding Diversification Strategy
consequences: Broadening financial sources reduces reliance on a single grant, but pursuing multiple streams may dilute focus and increase administrative overhead. External partners could influence study priorities.
options: Pursue a joint grant proposal with a regional health research council that aligns with the study’s public health impact objectives.
Develop a service‑based revenue model that offers anonymized data analytics to external sleep clinics under strict data use agreements.
Create a phased partnership with a pharmaceutical sponsor that funds specific sub‑studies while preserving independent publication rights.
review: Core tension: financial independence versus external influence. Weakness: The options do not consider the long‑term impact on research autonomy.

name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Framework
consequences: Integrating diverse expertise can accelerate discovery, yet coordination complexity may slow decision making and increase conflict potential. Misaligned incentives may hinder collaboration.
options: Establish a rotating joint‑lab week where neuroscientists, data engineers, and clinicians co‑design experiment protocols and troubleshoot data pipelines.
Implement a shared knowledge repository that documents best practices, code snippets, and clinical insights accessible to all team members.
Schedule quarterly interdisciplinary symposia that invite external experts to critique study design and propose novel analytical approaches.
review: Core tension: depth of expertise versus operational agility. Weakness: The options ignore potential misalignment of disciplinary incentives.

name: Participant Retention Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever will increase the likelihood that participants complete their full stay, reducing data loss. Higher retention may raise per‑participant costs and require more flexible scheduling. It also improves statistical power for longitudinal analyses.
options: Implement a personalized sleep diary feedback loop that shares nightly sleep summaries with participants, encouraging engagement and adherence.
Offer a tiered incentive program that rewards participants for consecutive nights of data capture, with escalating bonuses for full stays.
Schedule weekly virtual check-ins with a sleep coach to address concerns, reinforce study goals, and personalize comfort adjustments.
review: Core tension: balancing participant autonomy with study protocol adherence. Weakness: The options overlook potential cultural differences in engagement preferences.

name: Ethical Data Governance
consequences: Pulling this lever will formalize data stewardship, ensuring compliance with German privacy laws and participant trust. It may increase administrative overhead and slow data sharing timelines. It also sets a precedent for future studies on ethical handling of sensitive sleep data.
options: Establish a data governance board that includes patient advocates, legal experts, and researchers to oversee consent, access, and de‑identification processes.
Develop a dynamic consent platform that allows participants to modify data sharing preferences in real time, with automated audit trails.
Implement a zero‑knowledge proof protocol for data access that verifies researcher credentials without revealing participant identifiers.
review: Core tension: safeguarding participant privacy while enabling scientific collaboration. Weakness: The options do not address the administrative burden of implementing advanced cryptographic safeguards.

name: Technician Skill Development
consequences: Pulling this lever will elevate the technical proficiency of night staff, reducing annotation errors and improving event detection accuracy. It may require investment in training time and external expertise. It also fosters a culture of continuous learning that can attract higher‑quality hires.
options: Introduce a cross‑disciplinary bootcamp that pairs technicians with neurophysiology postdocs to deepen understanding of sleep architecture and artifact identification.
Deploy a peer‑review rotation where technicians annotate each other's recordings, providing immediate feedback and standardizing scoring practices.
Offer a certification pathway that rewards technicians for completing advanced courses in polysomnography and data annotation, with institutional recognition.
review: Core tension: balancing depth of technical expertise with staffing flexibility. Weakness: The options ignore the potential for skill attrition during high workload periods.

name: Environmental Comfort Calibration
consequences: Pulling this lever will fine‑tune room acoustics, lighting, and temperature to mimic natural sleep environments, potentially increasing data quality. It may require iterative testing and additional equipment, raising upfront costs. It also reduces the risk of participants feeling institutionalized, which could bias sleep behavior.
options: Conduct a series of blind sleep quality surveys after each renovation phase to quantify perceived comfort and correlate with event frequency.
Install adjustable smart blinds that automatically dim based on ambient light sensors, ensuring consistent darkness across all suites.
Integrate a low‑noise HVAC system that cycles on a randomized schedule, preventing predictable airflow patterns that could disturb sleep.
review: Core tension: achieving a naturalistic environment while maintaining safety and monitoring infrastructure. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of environmental changes on sensor performance.

name: Longitudinal Data Integration
consequences: Pulling this lever will streamline the merging of multimodal sensor streams into a unified analysis pipeline, accelerating research outputs. It may increase computational demands and require specialized data engineering resources. It also enhances reproducibility by standardizing data formats across time points.
options: Adopt a modular ETL framework that extracts raw sensor data, transforms it into BIDS‑compliant structures, and loads it into a central analytics database.
Implement a real‑time data validation layer that flags inconsistencies before storage, reducing downstream cleaning effort and preserving data integrity.
Create a version‑controlled annotation repository that tracks changes to event labels, enabling reproducible analyses and audit trails.
review: Core tension: balancing data integration speed with rigorous quality control. Weakness: The options do not consider the scalability of the chosen architecture for future expansion.

name: Funding Sustainability Planning
consequences: Pulling this lever will diversify revenue streams, reducing reliance on a single grant and enhancing long‑term viability. It may require additional administrative effort and risk of mission drift. It also positions the unit for potential industry collaborations while preserving academic independence.
options: Pursue a phased partnership with a medical device manufacturer that supplies compatible sensors, exchanging equipment for joint publication rights.
Launch a small‑scale educational outreach program that offers accredited sleep hygiene workshops to the local community, generating modest fee income.
Apply for a European Horizon grant focused on translational sleep research, leveraging the unit’s unique longitudinal data set as a core asset.
review: Core tension: securing financial stability while maintaining research integrity. Weakness: The options fail to address the administrative burden of managing multiple funding sources.
